items.prompt: In which novel does the lawyer Atticus Finch defend a Black man falsely accused of rape in a fictional Alabama town during the 1930s?
items.explanation:
Harper Lee's *To Kill a Mockingbird* (1960); narrated by Atticus's daughter Scout
Atticus Finch was ranked #1 on the American Film Institute's list of greatest movie heroes (Gregory Peck in the 1962 film)
Modeled partly on Lee's own father, Amasa Coleman Lee, also a lawyer in Monroeville, Alabama
Won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1961; sold over 40 million copies
The novel was meant to be Lee's only book; a second novel, *Go Set a Watchman* (2015), was written before Mockingbird but published late in Lee's life
items.cardFront: Atticus Finch (lawyer, Alabama, 1930s)

items.prompt: In which novel does the mysterious millionaire Jay Gatsby throw lavish parties at his Long Island mansion while pining for a married woman named Daisy Buchanan?
items.explanation:
F. Scott Fitzgerald's *The Great Gatsby* (1925); set in the fictional West Egg and East Egg on Long Island, 1922
Gatsby's real name is James Gatz; he reinvented himself and made his fortune through bootlegging
His obsessive love for Daisy Buchanan — symbolized by the green light across the bay — drives the novel
Gatsby is shot dead in his swimming pool by the husband of a woman his car struck; Daisy does not attend the funeral
'So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past' — the famous closing line
items.cardFront: Jay Gatsby (millionaire, Long Island parties, Daisy Buchanan)

items.prompt: This cynical, alienated 16-year-old narrator is expelled from his prep school and wanders New York City for three days, railing against the 'phoniness' of the adult world. In what novel does Holden Caulfield appear?
items.explanation:
J.D. Salinger, published 1951
Holden's narrative is retrospective — he is narrating from a rest home, after a breakdown, though this is barely acknowledged
The 'catcher in the rye' — Holden's fantasy of saving children from the cliff of adulthood — misquotes Robert Burns's 'Comin' Thro' the Rye'
Still sells ~250,000 copies per year; frequently challenged for profanity and sexual content
Found on Mark David Chapman when he shot John Lennon in 1980; also carried by John Hinckley Jr. at the Reagan assassination attempt
Salinger's identification with Holden was so intense that he published almost nothing after 1965 and became a famous recluse
items.cardFront: Holden Caulfield (expelled teenager, NYC, 'phonies')

items.prompt: This clever, wealthy, slightly meddlesome young woman in a Surrey village appoints herself matchmaker for everyone around her while remaining blind to her own feelings for her neighbor Mr. Knightley. In what novel does Emma Woodhouse appear?
items.explanation:
Jane Austen, published 1815
Austen wrote of Emma: 'I am going to take a heroine whom no one but myself will much like' — she is manipulative and self-deceiving
Mr. Knightley is the only character who criticizes Emma honestly — making him the moral anchor and eventual husband
The novel is a masterclass in free indirect discourse — the narrative reflects Emma's flawed perceptions as if they were fact
Mr. Woodhouse, Emma's hypochondriac father, is one of Austen's great comic characters
Adapted as *Clueless* (1995 film), set in a Beverly Hills high school — a faithful modern retelling
items.cardFront: Emma Woodhouse (matchmaker, Highbury, Austen)

items.prompt: This impoverished Russian student murders a pawnbroker to test his theory that extraordinary people are above conventional morality, then is tormented by guilt. In what novel does Raskolnikov appear?
items.explanation:
Fyodor Dostoevsky, published 1866
Rodion Raskolnikov — his name derives from the Russian word for 'schism' or 'split' (raskol)
He divides humanity into 'ordinary' people (who follow the law) and 'extraordinary' people (like Napoleon, who transcend it)
The investigating detective Porfiry Petrovich psychologically dismantles Raskolnikov through conversation, not evidence
Sonya Marmeladova — a prostitute who supports her destitute family — represents the redemptive power of suffering and faith
Dostoevsky wrote it under crushing debt, partly dictating to a stenographer to beat a contractual deadline
items.cardFront: Raskolnikov (student, Petersburg, murders pawnbroker)

items.prompt: This Dublin advertising canvasser of Hungarian-Jewish descent wanders the city on June 16, 1904, his thoughts following every detail of his day, in a novel structured as a modern *Odyssey*. In what novel does Leopold Bloom appear?
items.explanation:
James Joyce, published 1922
Bloom corresponds to Homer's Odysseus — he is the wanderer, husband, father figure
His wife Molly (Penelope) is having an affair with Blazes Boylan; Bloom knows and does nothing
Bloom's stream-of-consciousness is the novel's warmest and most accessible style
Bloom's Jewish identity is central — Joyce presents an outsider's Dublin with precision and sympathy
'Bloomsday' (June 16) is celebrated annually in Dublin with readings, re-enactments, and fancy dress
items.cardFront: Leopold Bloom (Dublin canvasser, June 16, 1904)

items.prompt: This dark-complexioned foundling, raised on the Yorkshire moors, devotes his life to an obsessive love for Catherine Earnshaw and revenge against all who scorned him. In what novel does Heathcliff appear?
items.explanation:
Emily Brontë, published 1847 under the pseudonym 'Ellis Bell' — Emily's only novel
Heathcliff's origins are deliberately mysterious — he may be Romani, Creole, or simply a foundling; his race is never specified
Catherine's famous declaration: 'I am Heathcliff!' — expressing a love that feels like identity itself
Kate Bush's 1978 debut single 'Wuthering Heights' hit #1 in the UK — written and recorded when Bush was 17
Shocked Victorian readers with its raw obsessive passion; initial reviews called it 'wild,' 'confused,' and 'disagreeable'
Now considered one of the greatest English novels; Heathcliff is often ranked literature's greatest anti-hero
items.cardFront: Heathcliff (foundling, obsessive love, Yorkshire moors)

items.prompt: In which Alexandre Dumas novel does the wrongly imprisoned sailor Edmond Dantès escape from the Château d'If, discover buried treasure, and take revenge as the mysterious Count of Monte Cristo?
items.explanation:
Alexandre Dumas's *The Count of Monte Cristo* (1844–1846); published serially in *Le Journal des Débats*
Dantès is imprisoned on a false charge of Bonapartism; in the Château d'If he meets Abbé Faria, who teaches him and reveals the location of hidden treasure
He adopts multiple disguises and identities to execute elaborate revenge on the three men who betrayed him
One of the best-selling novels of all time; Dumas wrote it with collaborator Auguste Maquet, a common 19th-century practice
'Wait and hope' — the novel's closing message to its young protagonist Maximilien
items.cardFront: Edmond Dantès (wrongfully imprisoned, revenge, Château d'If)

items.prompt: This Puritan woman in 17th-century Boston is forced to wear the letter 'A' on her gown as punishment for adultery, while her secret lover Reverend Dimmesdale suffers invisible torment. In what novel does Hester Prynne appear?
items.explanation:
Nathaniel Hawthorne, published 1850
Hester's 'A' (for Adultery) is embroidered elaborately — a defiant act of artistry that transforms punishment into art
The novel's three central symbols: the scarlet letter, the rosebush by the prison door, and the meteor in the sky
Hester eventually becomes a revered community counselor — the 'A' comes to stand for 'Able' in the town's eyes
Hawthorne (1804–1864) was descended from John Hathorne, a judge in the Salem witch trials; he added the 'w' to distance himself
The first major American novel; explored guilt, sin, and redemption in Puritan New England
items.cardFront: Hester Prynne (scarlet letter, Puritan Massachusetts, adultery)

items.prompt: In which Nabokov novel does the European scholar Humbert Humbert narrate his obsessive pursuit of the twelve-year-old 'nymphet' Dolores Haze?
items.explanation:
Vladimir Nabokov's *Lolita* (1955); one of the most controversial and most discussed novels of the 20th century
Nabokov makes Humbert a brilliantly unreliable narrator; his elegant prose is designed to seduce the reader alongside Lolita
Dolores's nickname 'Lolita' has entered English as a word for a sexually precocious girl — an interpretation Nabokov rejected as a misreading of his novel
Humbert marries Dolores's mother Charlotte to gain access to the child; Charlotte is subsequently killed by a car
The novel's famous last line: 'And this is the only immortality you and I may share, my Lolita'
items.cardFront: Humbert Humbert (unreliable narrator, Dolores 'Lolita' Haze)

items.prompt: This low-ranking Outer Party member secretly rebels against totalitarian Oceania by keeping a diary and having a forbidden love affair with a woman named Julia. In what novel does Winston Smith appear?
items.explanation:
George Orwell, published 1949
Winston works at the Ministry of Truth, where he rewrites historical records to match the Party's current version of events
His diary's opening line: 'April 4th, 1984. Last night to the flicks' — mundane notation in a monstrous world
'Room 101' — the torture chamber containing each prisoner's worst personal fear — becomes a common cultural reference
O'Brien, the Party official Winston trusts as a secret rebel, is revealed as his torturer — betrayal as the ultimate Orwellian act
'If you want a picture of the future, imagine a boot stamping on a human face — forever' — O'Brien explains the Party's goal
items.cardFront: Winston Smith (Ministry of Truth, Big Brother, Party)

items.prompt: This formerly enslaved woman in post-Civil War Ohio is haunted by the ghost of the infant daughter she killed to spare her from slavery, and by a mysterious young woman who calls herself Beloved. In what novel does Sethe appear?
items.explanation:
Toni Morrison, published 1987; Pulitzer Prize 1988
Sethe's act is presented without condemnation — Morrison asks readers to understand the logic of slavery's devastation
Inspired by the true case of Margaret Garner (1856), an enslaved woman who killed her daughter rather than let her be recaptured
Beloved's identity is deliberately ambiguous — ghost, embodied spirit, survivor, or something else
The novel opens with a dedication: 'Sixty Million and more' — those who died in the Middle Passage and slavery
Morrison said the novel was an act of re-memory: returning the erased to historical consciousness
items.cardFront: Sethe (formerly enslaved, Ohio, haunted by Beloved)

items.prompt: This witty, independent Regency-era woman from Hertfordshire sparring with the proud Mr. Darcy gradually discovers she has misjudged him as profoundly as he has misjudged her. In what novel does Elizabeth Bennet appear?
items.explanation:
Jane Austen, published 1813
Elizabeth is the second of five Bennet daughters; her mother's desperate matchmaking is a constant source of humiliation
Austen described Elizabeth as 'the most delightful creature as ever appeared in print' in a letter to her sister Cassandra
Darcy's first (rejected) proposal is the pivot of the novel — she rejects him; he revisits himself; both are transformed
Colin Firth's portrayal in the 1995 BBC miniseries created a cultural phenomenon (and the famous wet-shirt scene)
The novel has been adapted 9+ times for film and TV, plus retellings including *Bridget Jones's Diary* (1996)
items.cardFront: Elizabeth Bennet (witty heroine, Mr. Darcy, five sisters)

items.prompt: This married Russian aristocrat abandons her husband and son for a passionate affair with the dashing Count Vronsky, only to be destroyed by social condemnation and her own jealousy. In what novel does Anna Karenina appear?
items.explanation:
Leo Tolstoy, published 1878
Anna's story is tragic; the parallel Levin plot (a landowner seeking meaning through farming and marriage) ends hopefully
Anna throws herself under a train at a Moscow station — a death foreshadowed from the novel's opening
Tolstoy reportedly felt deep ambivalence about Anna; she kept going in directions he didn't intend
She is condemned by society not for adultery itself, but for openly pursuing what others pursue secretly
'Vengeance is mine; I will repay' (Romans 12:19) is the novel's epigraph — whose vengeance? God's? Society's? Anna's own?
items.cardFront: Anna Karenina (aristocrat, Count Vronsky, railway suicide)

items.prompt: This traveling salesman wakes one morning to find he has transformed into a monstrous insect, exploring themes of alienation, family burden, and dehumanization. In what novella does Gregor Samsa appear?
items.explanation:
Franz Kafka, published 1915
The opening line: 'One morning, when Gregor Samsa woke from troubled dreams, he found himself transformed in his bed into a horrible vermin'
The exact nature of the vermin is deliberately unspecified — Kafka refused to let the cover illustration show any insect
Gregor's family initially attempts to care for him, then treats him with growing disgust and indifference
After Gregor's death, his family feels relieved — they take a pleasant tram ride and plan for the future
Kafka's metaphor for how capitalism and family obligation can strip a person of humanity
items.cardFront: Gregor Samsa (transformed into insect, burden to family, Kafka)

items.prompt: This barefoot Missouri boy runs away from his abusive father and floats down the Mississippi River on a raft with an escaped slave named Jim. In what novel does Huckleberry Finn appear?
items.explanation:
Mark Twain, published 1884–1885
Huck's moral journey is the novel's core: he decides to 'go to hell' rather than turn Jim in — a rejection of society's false morality
Hemingway declared this novel the source of 'all modern American literature'
The novel's vernacular voice — Huck's dialect and perspective — was revolutionary; previous American fiction used 'literary' language
Famously banned almost immediately after publication for its use of racial slurs and depictions of violence
Jim is one of American literature's most complex Black characters — dignified, morally superior to his 'civilized' captors
items.cardFront: Huckleberry Finn (runaway boy, Jim, Mississippi River raft)

items.prompt: In which Henry James novel does the independent American heiress Isabel Archer travel to Europe and fall prey to the scheming Gilbert Osmond?
items.explanation:
Henry James's *The Portrait of a Lady* (1881); considered his first major masterpiece
Isabel is from Albany, New York; she inherits a fortune and goes to Europe, believing she can shape her own destiny
Gilbert Osmond is a cold aesthete living in Florence; his manipulation of Isabel is revealed gradually to the reader
The novel ends ambiguously: Isabel chooses to return to Osmond, reasons debated by scholars for over a century
James's style — long sentences, psychological interiority, understated revelation — defines the 'International Theme' (Americans in Europe)
items.cardFront: Isabel Archer (American heiress, Europe, James)

items.prompt: In which Hugo novel does the ex-convict Jean Valjean, originally imprisoned for stealing bread, spend decades evading the relentless Inspector Javert while transforming himself into a benefactor?
items.explanation:
Victor Hugo's *Les Misérables* (1862); one of the great novels of French literature
Valjean is imprisoned 19 years (5 for stealing bread, 14 for escape attempts) and becomes a fugitive under the alias Monsieur Madeleine
The novel covers the political turmoil of Restoration France (1815–1832) and the Paris student uprising of June 1832
Javert, the police inspector who pursues Valjean, drowns himself when his worldview of absolute law collapses
Fantine, Éponine, and Cosette are the novel's major female characters; Fantine's story is among its most powerful
Adapted as a musical by Alain Boublil and Claude-Michel Schönberg (Paris, 1980; London, 1985), one of the longest-running musicals
items.cardFront: Jean Valjean (ex-convict, Inspector Javert, Paris)

items.prompt: In which Heller novel does the bombardier John Yossarian desperately try to get grounded from WWII missions in Italy, only to be trapped by the rule that if you're sane enough to want out, you're sane enough to fly?
items.explanation:
Joseph Heller's *Catch-22* (1961); set on the fictional island of Pianosa off the Italian coast during WWII
The 'catch-22' paradox: a pilot can be grounded for insanity, but requesting to be grounded proves he is sane, so he must fly
The term 'catch-22' entered English as a common phrase for an inescapable paradoxical dilemma
Heller flew 60 actual combat missions as a bombardier in WWII; the bureaucratic absurdities are drawn from experience
The novel sold modestly at first but became a cult classic during the Vietnam War as anti-war satire
items.cardFront: Yossarian (WWII bombardier, Italy, trapped by paradox)

items.prompt: In which Toole novel does the obese, pompous medievalist Ignatius J. Reilly bumble through 1960s New Orleans accompanied by his valve, his Big Chief notebooks, and his disdain for all modernity?
items.explanation:
John Kennedy Toole's *A Confederacy of Dunces* (published posthumously 1980); Toole died by suicide in 1969 at age 31
His mother Thelma Toole spent 11 years trying to get the novel published; finally succeeded with Louisiana State University Press
Won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1981 — one of only two Pulitzer winners published posthumously
Ignatius quotes Boethius's *Consolation of Philosophy* constantly; his 'pyloric valve' closes whenever he encounters anything he disapproves of
Considered the funniest American novel; set in New Orleans's French Quarter, which Toole knew intimately
items.cardFront: Ignatius J. Reilly (pompous medievalist, New Orleans, big valve)

items.prompt: This monomaniacal whaling captain lost his leg to a white sperm whale and leads his crew on a fatal obsessive hunt across the oceans. In what novel does Captain Ahab appear?
items.explanation:
Herman Melville, published 1851
Ahab's peg leg ('ivory leg') is carved from a whale's jaw — he is physically marked by his obsession
He appears only in Act II of the novel; the first part establishes the world of whaling before Ahab commands the stage
'From hell's heart I stab at thee; for hate's sake I spit my last breath at thee' — Ahab's final words to the whale
Ahab's name alludes to the biblical King Ahab — the wicked king of Israel in 1 Kings 16
Ahab's fixed nature contrasts with Ishmael's flexible observation — Ahab seeks final truth; Ishmael accepts ambiguity
items.cardFront: Captain Ahab (whaling captain, peg leg, white whale)

items.prompt: This wealthy, jilted woman stopped all the clocks in her mansion at the moment she was stood up on her wedding day, and still wears her rotting bridal gown decades later. In what novel does Miss Havisham appear?
items.explanation:
Charles Dickens, published 1860–1861
Her wedding cake still sits on the table, now a mass of cobwebs and decay — a stopped-time monument to trauma
She adopts Estella and deliberately raises her to break men's hearts, as revenge for her own heartbreak
'I have sick fancies' — Miss Havisham's self-awareness of her obsession makes her more tragic than villainous
Ends the novel having accidentally set herself on fire from the decayed wedding dress; dies of burns and guilt
Freudian in structure: Miss Havisham is one of the earliest fictional examinations of how trauma arrests development
items.cardFront: Miss Havisham (jilted bride, stopped clocks, rotting gown)

items.prompt: This beautiful young man wishes his portrait would age instead of him, gets his wish, and pursues a life of hedonism and wickedness while remaining forever youthful. In what novel does Dorian Gray appear?
items.explanation:
Oscar Wilde, published 1890 in *Lippincott's Monthly Magazine*; revised for book publication 1891
The portrait is hidden in a locked attic room; Dorian can see his moral corruption accumulating in the painting while his face stays young
Lord Henry Wotton is the witty, amoral influence on Dorian — often read as a projection of Wilde himself
The novel was used against Wilde at his 1895 trial for gross indecency as evidence of 'immoral' tendencies
Wilde (1854–1900) died in Paris two years after his release from prison; reportedly said 'I am dying beyond my means'
The Faustian bargain (selling the soul for worldly gain) underpins the plot; no devil appears, only aesthetic philosophy
items.cardFront: Dorian Gray (eternal youth, aging portrait, Wilde)

items.prompt: This Spanish gentleman reads so many chivalric romances that he loses his mind, dons rusty armor, and sets out as a knight-errant with his squire Sancho Panza, famously tilting at windmills he believes are giants. In what novel does Don Quixote appear?
alternates: El Ingenioso Hidalgo Don Quijote de la Mancha
items.explanation:
Miguel de Cervantes, published in two parts (1605 and 1615); often called the first modern novel
Don Quixote's real name is Alonso Quixano; 'Don Quixote de la Mancha' is his self-invented chivalric identity
Sancho Panza, the earthy peasant squire, grounds Quixote's delusions in comic reality — the novel's great double act
The windmill episode (tilting at giants) became a universal metaphor for idealistic struggle against imaginary enemies
Cervantes lost the use of his left hand at the Battle of Lepanto (1571) against the Ottoman fleet
The most translated book in the world after the Bible, by some counts; has sold hundreds of millions of copies
items.cardFront: Don Quixote (knight-errant, Sancho Panza, windmills)
